reasoning:
The ACLED baseline reference value of 1.000 on December 11 indicates that exactly zero 'Protest' events were recorded in Algeria over the preceding 360 days. To trigger a YES resolution, a 30-day leading period would need to see more than 10 times this baseline (i.e. at least 11 protests) in a single month. Achieving an increase from 0 protests per year to 11+ per month would be an unprecedented spike requiring a massive social upheaval. 

Recent search findings indicate that the Algerian government maintains a very tight grip on dissent, effectively crushing offline organization and arresting those who speak out online. For example, recent online calls for a "GenZ213" demonstration in October 2025 seemingly failed to generate any offline protests recorded by ACLED. 

For the first window (Dec 28, 2025), roughly 23 days of the 30-day window have already passed with no breaking news of major unrest in Algeria, making an 11+ protest surge practically impossible. For the second window (Jan 20, 2026), the structural barriers to organizing protests remain intense. For the final window (Mar 21, 2026), the forecast horizon is further out, and the February anniversary of the Hirak movement along with the approach of mid-2026 parliamentary elections introduces a bit more uncertainty, slightly increasing the likelihood of sporadic protests. However, a massive 11+ event spike remains highly unlikely across all windows.
